results.possibleIdentity: Juliette Gordon (born 1934) is a New York-based feminist artist, writer, and activist who was a central figure in the radical art movements of the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is best known as a founding member of Women Artists in Revolution (W.A.R.), established in 1969 to protest the underrepresentation of women in museums and galleries. Gordon was also deeply involved in the Art Workers Coalition (A.W.C.) and radical anti-war politics, notably demanding a "women's wing" at the Museum of Modern Art during an Art Workers Union meeting. Her artistic practice spanned collage, photocollage, assemblage, painting, drawing, and printmaking, often characterized by a witty and political edge. In 1968, she worked on large-scale assemblages in Yoko Ono's loft at 112 Chambers Street. She held numerous solo exhibitions at the Star Turtle Gallery, Alan Stone Gallery, and her own Juliette Gordon Gallery on East 73rd Street, and participated in the 1972 'Ten Downtown' artist-run studio initiative. In 1971, her work was featured in the 17th biennial print exhibit at the Brooklyn Museum. Although she was a prominent voice in the early feminist art movement, she was considered a 'lost pioneer' for many years until being re-recognized by organizations like the Veteran Feminists of America.
